pathophysiology_summary: Urticaria is mast-cell histamine release causing transient, intensely pruritic, blanching wheals with central pallor and a red flare, individually lasting <24 hours and migrating across the body — here likely NSAID (ibuprofen)-triggered. It is a superficial dermal reaction and, when isolated to the skin (no airway/mucosal angioedema, no wheeze, no hypotension/GI features), is benign; but urticaria can be the first sign of an evolving anaphylaxis, so the airway/circulation must be actively screened and strongly safety-netted. Discriminators: transient migratory blanching wheals distinguish urticaria from the fixed (days-long) three-zone target lesions of erythema multiforme, and from the localised, fixed, scaly/vesicular pattern of contact dermatitis; the absence of lip/tongue/throat swelling, wheeze, hypotension and GI symptoms distinguishes isolated urticaria from anaphylaxis. Management is a high-dose non-sedating antihistamine (regularly, up-titrated), ceasing the trigger (NSAIDs), cool measures, and an explicit anaphylaxis safety-net (adrenaline auto-injector/000 for any airway, breathing or circulatory symptom).

differential.evidence_basis: Migratory blanching pale-centred wheals fading within hours, NSAID exposure.
Urticaria can herald anaphylaxis — actively screen airway/breathing/circulation.
Fixed three-zone targets lasting days, not migratory wheals.
Localised, fixed, scaly/vesicular — not migratory blanching wheals.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Screen for anaphylaxis/angioedema FIRST; if skin-only, treat urticaria with high-dose antihistamine + trigger cessation + a strong anaphylaxis safety-net.

differential.evidence_basis: Migratory blanching wheals; no airway/mucosal/circulatory features.
No angioedema/wheeze/hypotension/GI symptoms — remains the key safety-net.
Working diagnosis: high-dose non-sedating antihistamine + cease NSAIDs + cool measures + anaphylaxis safety-net.

red_flags.description: Anaphylaxis/angioedema features: lip/tongue/throat swelling, wheeze/breathlessness, faintness/hypotension, vomiting
red_flags.clinical_significance: Anaphylaxis is life-threatening and needs immediate adrenaline; urticaria can evolve into it.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Standard single-dose rather than regular high-dose non-sedating antihistamine for severe urticaria.
Not identifying/ceasing the likely NSAID trigger.

telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: If skin-only on screening, high-dose non-sedating antihistamine + cease NSAIDs + cool measures; explicit anaphylaxis safety-net (adrenaline/000 for any airway/breathing/faintness).
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Missing evolving anaphylaxis, or under-treating severe urticaria.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Acute urticaria: regular non-sedating antihistamine (up-titrate to high dose if needed); identify/avoid triggers; provide an anaphylaxis action plan and safety-net; adrenaline for anaphylaxis.
